narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, you've put significant effort into your law school application, including your LSAT score of 36 and undergraduate GPA of 3.0, while planning to attend full-time. The admissions model we use predicted a 61.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above the threshold we set for approving applications—meaning your application was unfortunately rejected to minimize the risk of future bar failure. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic decile rankings from school years 1 and 3, both at the 3rd decile, which are notably lower than the typical student's 6th decile or higher. These early and later grade positions stood out as the strongest factors pushing the model toward forecasting a higher risk of bar failure, well below what we've seen in successful applicants. Your LSAT score, while solid at 36 and close to the average of 36.7, helped counterbalance this somewhat by nudging the prediction toward success, showing your potential in standardized testing. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 3.0 provided a modest positive influence, though it fell short of the dataset average of 3.21 seen in those who typically pass the bar. Your family income bracket and full-time enrollment plans had smaller roles, slightly tilting toward higher risk but not as dominantly as your deciles. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your profile shows stronger test scores but weaker relative school performance, which the model weighed heavily in this case. I know this rejection stings, especially given your dedication, and it's okay to feel disappointed—many strong candidates face this hurdle. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through targeted GPA improvement or additional certifications that highlight your analytical strengths like your LSAT. You might also explore part-time options or gap-year experiences to build a more competitive academic record. We're rooting for you to strengthen these areas and reapply stronger—law schools value persistence from determined applicants like you. Keep pushing; your story isn't over yet.
